content.full_text:
· SACRAMENTO, 95804 '" 
1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Stephen E. Smith 
Executive Director 
July 6, 1976 
Commission for Economic Development 
107 South Broadway, Room 4007 
Los Angeles, California 
90012 
Dear Mr. 
Smith~ 
Thank you for your letter of June 22, 1976, inquiring 
as to whether advisory members of the Commission for Economic 
Development have any duties or responsibilities for disclo-
sure under Proposition Nine. 
A summary review of your enabling legislation suggests 
that your Commission possesses decision-making authority. 
The legislative members of the Commission constitute a joint 
investigatory committee (Government Code §14999.1) with the 
right to employ clerical, legal and technical staff, subject 
to authorization by the joint rules committee; the authority 
to hold hearings, administer oaths, take testimony, issue 
summons, subpoena witnesses and/or documents, and compel 
attendance and testimony (Joint Rule 36., Investigatory 
Committees). 
In addition, the Cormnission, chaired by the 
Lieutenant Governor, has the power to contract for studies 
and other special services to carry out the statutory func-
tions of the Co~~ission (Government Code §14999.2). 
The 
authority to select and initiate studies and to hire staff 
to perform these studies is quite broad (Government Code 
§14999.8). 
Given these facts, the members of your Commis-
sion would seem to be "public officials" within the meaning 
of 2 Cal.Adm. Code §18700 (a) (1) (A). 
As "public officials", 
they are subject to the general conflict of interest prohi-
bition contained in Government Code §87100: 
87100. 
Public Officials; State and Local. 
No 
public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or 
in any way attempt to use his official position 
to influence a governmental decision in which he 
knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest. 
TEC: l~;;C.AL A 'SSISTANCE! ADMINISTRATION (916) 322~5660 
0 
EXECUTIVE )lEGAL 
322~5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 3226441 


· 
I 
'Hr. Stephen E. Smith 
July 6, 1976 
Page Two 
I attach a copy of the above-cited regulation for your review. 
Are the members of your Commission also "designated 
employees", and as such, subject to the disclosure provisions 
of an agency Conflict of Interest Code, either your own Code, 
or that of the Office of the Lieutenant Governor? 
A summary 
review of the statutory authority cited above suggests that 
the Commission may well be required to adopt its own Conflict 
of Interest Code if it is not included within the Code of the 
Lieutenant Governor. 
Richard Thompson, who is preparing the 
Conflict of Interest Code for the Lieutenant Governor's Office, 
indicates that the Commission is not currently included, but, 
subject to your wishes, could be included. 
You might wish to 
discuss this issue with him and to review the provisions of 
Government Code §§87300-8730~and §82019, to consider whether 
you are an agency that has any "designated employees" (includ-
ing the members of your Co~~ission) . 
If you are not included within the Conflict of Interest 
Code of the Lieutenant Governor, we will be sending you a 
separate notice of duty to submit a Conflict of Interest Code, 
pursuant to the provisions of Government Code §87300, shortly. 
If, after your review, however, you feel that you have no 
"designated employees", I would urge that you request an 
exemption from the requirement to submit a proposed Conflict 
of Interest Code. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission is, 
this week, considering adoption of a procedure for requesting 
exemption from the requirement of adopting a Conflict of 
Interest Code. 
One ground for such request is that the agency 
concerned has no "designated employees". 
The other ground is 
that the agency is no longer operative and functioning, or 
soon will no longer be operative and functioning. 
You might 
wish to base your request on either or both grounds. 
I 
attach a copy of the proposed regulation for your consideration. 
If I may be of further assistance to you, please let me 
know. 
EAK/g 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Edgar A. Kerry 
Staff Attorney 
Conflicts of Interest 
Division 


June 22, 1976 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
926 J Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Ladies and Gentlemen: 
ENT 
! 400 TENTH STREET, ROOM 109 
SACRAMENTO, CA 956 
(91 
) 445·1025 
BROADWAY, ROOM 4007 
Q{ 
Los ANGELES, CA 900! 
(213) 620·2560 
The Commission for Economic Development is a state agency 
established by Sections 14999 et seq of the Government Code. 
At its meeting of May 24th the Commission voted to invite two 
persons to become "Advisory Members" of the Commission. 
It 
is our intention to add additional advisory members from time 
to time in the future. 
Basic criterion for selection of an 
advisory member is that he or she holds an important position 
in a business or industry concerned with economic development 
ln California. 
As we read the Government Code the functions of the Commission 
are entirely advisory in character, although the Commission 
has certain ancilary functions (to confer, to contract, etc). 
Under the procedures adopted by the Commission advisory 
members have full rights to participate in the discussions 
of the Commission but may not vote. 
Formal recorded votes 
are unusual but not completely unheard of. 
Assuming the descriptions contained in this letter are accurate, 
do advisory members of the Commission have any duties or 
responsibilities for reporting or disclosure under Proposition 9. 
The favor of a prompt response would be appreciated. 
If this 
question is completely free of doubt, a staff review and 
response will be satisfactory. 
STEVEN E. SMITH 
Executive Director 
SES:yms 
cc: 
Honorable Mervyn M. Dymally

STEVEN E. SMITH 
Executive Director

Honorable Mervyn M. Dymally
sections.conclusion: Given these facts, the members of your Commission would seem to be "public officials" within the meaning of 2 Cal.Adm. Code §18700 (a) (1) (A). As "public officials", they are subject to the general conflict of interest prohibition contained in Government Code §87100. The Commission may well be required to adopt its own Conflict of Interest Code if it is not included within the Code of the Lieutenant Governor. If not included within the Lieutenant Governor's Code, the Commission will receive notice of duty to submit a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code §87300.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether advisory members of the Commission for Economic Development have disclosure duties or responsibilities under Proposition 9 (the Political Reform Act).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commission members qualify as "public officials" subject to conflict of interest prohibitions under Government Code §87100, and the Commission likely must either be included in the Lieutenant Governor's Conflict of Interest Code or adopt its own Code under Government Code §87300.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (June 22, 1976 letter from Smith) and FPPC response (July 6, 1976 letter from Kerry). This is the FPPC response letter. Question and conclusion sections clearly identified in the narrative format. Enclosures referenced but not included in provided text.

QUESTION: is completely free of doubt, a staff review and 
response will be satisfactory. 
STEVEN E. SMITH 
Executive Director 
SES:yms 
cc: 
Honorable Mervyn M. Dymally

CONCLUSION: Given these facts, the members of your Commission would seem to be "public officials" within the meaning of 2 Cal.Adm. Code §18700 (a) (1) (A). As "public officials", they are subject to the general conflict of interest prohibition contained in Government Code §87100. The Commission may well be required to adopt its own Conflict of Interest Code if it is not included within the Code of the Lieutenant Governor. If not included within the Lieutenant Governor's Code, the Commission will receive notice of duty to submit a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code §87300.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises the Commission for Economic Development that its advisory members constitute "public officials" with decision-making authority subject to Proposition 9 conflict of interest disclosure requirements. The Commission should either be included in the Lieutenant Governor's Conflict of Interest Code or adopt its own Code if it has "designated employees."
